Long ago, the night forest belonged to absolute darkness. The stars, arrogant and cold, refused to share their brilliance with the creatures below, hoarding their silver light in the velvet sky. Nocturnal animals wandered blindly, constantly stumbling over roots and stones.

A small, drab beetle named Lumo pitied his fellow creatures. Determined to help, Lumo spent a week climbing the Great Oak, the tallest tree in the world, whose upper branches scraped the heavens. When he reached the canopy, he begged the stars for just a single crumb of illumination. The stars only laughed. Their cruel, booming mirth shook the cosmos, dislodging a shower of tiny, glowing specks of stardust.

Knowing the damp earth would extinguish the embers instantly, Lumo leapt from the branch. He darted through the night air, catching the falling fragments in his own belly. The celestial magic did not burn him; instead, it fused with his shell, turning his abdomen into a brilliant, pulsing lantern.

Touched by the little beetle's selfless bravery, the gentle moon blessed Lumo and his kin. They became the fireflies, eternal guardians of the undergrowth, forever carrying fragments of fallen stars to guide lost travelers through the shadowed woods.

Long ago, before the high mountains had names, there lived a spirit of the stony peaks named Aella. Unlike the bold gods of the rushing wind or the proud deities of the blazing sun, Aella was impossibly shy. She possessed a singing voice of unparalleled crystalline beauty, yet she hid deep within the dark caverns, terrified of being seen by mortal eyes.

One autumn twilight, a young shepherd wandered into her valley, desperately searching for a lost lamb. Exhausted, he sat upon a mossy rock and began to play a mournful tune on his wooden flute. Aella, captivated by the sweet melody, hummed along from the shadows. The shepherd stopped his playing, startled. "Who is there?" he called out into the gathering dark.

Aella’s heart fluttered wildly. She wanted to step forward, to declare her presence and comfort the lonely mortal. But her courage ultimately failed her. Instead of speaking her own thoughts, she nervously whispered back his final words: "Who is there?"

"Show yourself!" he cried.
"Show yourself!" she replied, her voice trembling.

The shepherd eventually left, but Aella never stopped listening. To this day, the shy spirit hides in the rocky crevices, answering travelers.
